chapters.title: Tutorial: The Shape of Go Programs
chapters.description: Start with tiny complete programs: input, transformation, output.

chapters.title: Program Structure
chapters.description: Names, declarations, scope, packages, and zero values.

chapters.title: Basic Data Types
chapters.description: Booleans, numbers, strings, constants, bytes, and runes.

chapters.title: Composite Types
chapters.description: Arrays, slices, maps, structs, and JSON-shaped data.

chapters.title: Functions
chapters.description: Function design, errors, recursion, closures, panic/recover, and defer.

chapters.title: Methods
chapters.description: Receivers, method sets, encapsulation-by-convention, and reusable data behavior.

chapters.title: Interfaces
chapters.description: Small behavior contracts, sorting, readers/writers, type assertions, and polymorphism.

chapters.title: Shared Variables and Mutexes
chapters.description: Race-free shared state, mutexes, once-only init, and confinement.

chapters.title: Packages and the Go Tool
chapters.description: Package design, imports, internal APIs, modules, and build/test workflows.

chapters.title: Testing
chapters.description: Table tests, benchmarks, examples, coverage thinking, and testable design.

chapters.title: Reflection
chapters.description: Inspecting dynamic values, tags, generic encoders, and reflect pitfalls.

chapters.title: Low-Level Programming
chapters.description: Unsafe, memory layout, alignment, byte views, and when not to be clever.

problems.title: Hello, Gopher
problems.statement: Return the exact greeting string for a supplied name.
problems.explanation:
Intuition first: this problem is asking you to model one tiny transformation. The input is not mysterious; it is just data waiting to be reshaped into the return value.

A good Go solution here avoids hiding the important state. If you need a running answer, name it something like total, out, seen, or best. If you need to remember membership, use a map. If you need to preserve order, keep a slice. If you need to inspect text, decide whether byte-level or rune-level processing is correct.

The reference solution is short because the data structure does most of the work. Read it from top to bottom and track exactly how the state changes for the first example. That trace is the real explanation.

Why this is idiomatic Go: concrete types, explicit control flow, useful zero values, and small helper logic. If your solution has more ceremony than the problem statement, simplify it.
hints: Say the function signature in English before coding.
Write the zero/empty case first.
Trace the first sample manually and make your variables follow that trace.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(name string) string { return "Hello, "+name+"!" }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve("Ada"), "Hello, Ada!"); assertEqual(Solve("Go"), "Hello, Go!") }
problems.lesson:
This exercise belongs to Tutorial: The Shape of Go Programs.

The big idea: Start with tiny complete programs: input, transformation, output.

Do not treat this like random syntax practice. Treat it like learning a mental move. Every Go problem has three visible things: the data coming in, the state you keep while working, and the data going out. If you can name those three, the code usually becomes short.

For this problem, stare at the function signature first. The parameter types tell you what world you are living in. A []int means order matters. A map means lookup/counting matters. A string means you must ask: bytes or runes? A struct means fields have meaning.

Your study goal: solve it once slowly and clearly. Then solve it again with fewer moving parts. Idiomatic Go usually feels calm: small variables, explicit loops, boring names, and no magic.
problems.approach:
Attack plan for Hello, Gopher:

1. Identify the zero/empty case before writing the main logic.
2. Choose the smallest useful state variable. Do not create a framework for a 10-line function.
3. Walk through one sample by hand and make your variable changes match that story.
4. Only after it works, ask if the code reads like plain English.

For this specific task, the core move is: Return the exact greeting string for a supplied name. Keep the implementation direct; the judge rewards correctness and clarity, not cleverness.
pitfalls: Starting with code before understanding the input/output shape.
Using a clever abstraction where a plain loop or map would be clearer.
Skipping edge cases: empty input, one item, duplicates, negative numbers, or Unicode depending on the problem.

problems.title: Temperature Table
problems.statement: Convert a list of Fahrenheit temperatures to Celsius, rounded toward zero.
problems.explanation:
Intuition first: this problem is asking you to model one tiny transformation. The input is not mysterious; it is just data waiting to be reshaped into the return value.

A good Go solution here avoids hiding the important state. If you need a running answer, name it something like total, out, seen, or best. If you need to remember membership, use a map. If you need to preserve order, keep a slice. If you need to inspect text, decide whether byte-level or rune-level processing is correct.

The reference solution is short because the data structure does most of the work. Read it from top to bottom and track exactly how the state changes for the first example. That trace is the real explanation.

Why this is idiomatic Go: concrete types, explicit control flow, useful zero values, and small helper logic. If your solution has more ceremony than the problem statement, simplify it.
hints: Say the function signature in English before coding.
Write the zero/empty case first.
Trace the first sample manually and make your variables follow that trace.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(f []int) []int { out:=make([]int,len(f)); for i,x:=range f { out[i]=(x-32)*5/9 }; return out }
problems.lesson:

Skipping edge cases: empty input, one item, duplicates, negative numbers, or Unicode depending on the problem.

problems.title: Unique Lines
problems.statement: Given lines of text, return the first occurrence of each line preserving order.
problems.explanation:
Intuition first: this problem is asking you to model one tiny transformation. The input is not mysterious; it is just data waiting to be reshaped into the return value.

A good Go solution here avoids hiding the important state. If you need a running answer, name it something like total, out, seen, or best. If you need to remember membership, use a map. If you need to preserve order, keep a slice. If you need to inspect text, decide whether byte-level or rune-level processing is correct.

The reference solution is short because the data structure does most of the work. Read it from top to bottom and track exactly how the state changes for the first example. That trace is the real explanation.

Why this is idiomatic Go: concrete types, explicit control flow, useful zero values, and small helper logic. If your solution has more ceremony than the problem statement, simplify it.
hints: Say the function signature in English before coding.
Write the zero/empty case first.
Trace the first sample manually and make your variables follow that trace.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(lines []string) []string { seen:=map[string]bool{}; out:=[]string{}; for _,s:=range lines { if !seen[s] {seen[s]=true; out=append(out,s)} }; return out }

problems.title: Byte Popcount
problems.statement: Count the number of set bits in an unsigned 8-bit integer.
problems.explanation:
Intuition first: this problem is asking you to model one tiny transformation. The input is not mysterious; it is just data waiting to be reshaped into the return value.

A good Go solution here avoids hiding the important state. If you need a running answer, name it something like total, out, seen, or best. If you need to remember membership, use a map. If you need to preserve order, keep a slice. If you need to inspect text, decide whether byte-level or rune-level processing is correct.

The reference solution is short because the data structure does most of the work. Read it from top to bottom and track exactly how the state changes for the first example. That trace is the real explanation.

Why this is idiomatic Go: concrete types, explicit control flow, useful zero values, and small helper logic. If your solution has more ceremony than the problem statement, simplify it.
hints: Say the function signature in English before coding.
Write the zero/empty case first.
Trace the first sample manually and make your variables follow that trace.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(x uint8) int { c:=0; for x>0 { c+=int(x&1); x >>= 1 }; return c }

problems.title: Shadow Sum
problems.statement: Simulate block-scoped updates: for each number add it to sum; if it is even, add it again as a temporary bonus.
problems.explanation:
Intuition first: this problem is asking you to model one tiny transformation. The input is not mysterious; it is just data waiting to be reshaped into the return value.

A good Go solution here avoids hiding the important state. If you need a running answer, name it something like total, out, seen, or best. If you need to remember membership, use a map. If you need to preserve order, keep a slice. If you need to inspect text, decide whether byte-level or rune-level processing is correct.

The reference solution is short because the data structure does most of the work. Read it from top to bottom and track exactly how the state changes for the first example. That trace is the real explanation.

Why this is idiomatic Go: concrete types, explicit control flow, useful zero values, and small helper logic. If your solution has more ceremony than the problem statement, simplify it.
hints: Say the function signature in English before coding.
Write the zero/empty case first.
Trace the first sample manually and make your variables follow that trace.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(nums []int) int { sum:=0; for _,x:=range nums { sum+=x; if x%2==0 { y:=x; sum+=y } }; return sum }

problems.title: Comma Format
problems.statement: Format a non-negative integer string with commas every three digits.
problems.explanation:
Intuition first: this problem is asking you to model one tiny transformation. The input is not mysterious; it is just data waiting to be reshaped into the return value.

A good Go solution here avoids hiding the important state. If you need a running answer, name it something like total, out, seen, or best. If you need to remember membership, use a map. If you need to preserve order, keep a slice. If you need to inspect text, decide whether byte-level or rune-level processing is correct.

The reference solution is short because the data structure does most of the work. Read it from top to bottom and track exactly how the state changes for the first example. That trace is the real explanation.

Why this is idiomatic Go: concrete types, explicit control flow, useful zero values, and small helper logic. If your solution has more ceremony than the problem statement, simplify it.
hints: Say the function signature in English before coding.
Write the zero/empty case first.
Trace the first sample manually and make your variables follow that trace.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(s string) string { n:=len(s); if n<=3 {return s}; r:=n%3; out:=""; if r>0 {out=s[:r]; if r<n {out+=","}}; for i:=r;i<n;i+=3 { out+=s[i:i+3]; if i+3<n {out+=","} }; return out }
problems.lesson:
This exercise belongs to Basic Data Types.

problems.title: Unicode Anagram
problems.statement: Return true if two strings contain the same runes with the same multiplicities.
problems.explanation:
Intuition first: this problem is asking you to model one tiny transformation. The input is not mysterious; it is just data waiting to be reshaped into the return value.

A good Go solution here avoids hiding the important state. If you need a running answer, name it something like total, out, seen, or best. If you need to remember membership, use a map. If you need to preserve order, keep a slice. If you need to inspect text, decide whether byte-level or rune-level processing is correct.

The reference solution is short because the data structure does most of the work. Read it from top to bottom and track exactly how the state changes for the first example. That trace is the real explanation.

Why this is idiomatic Go: concrete types, explicit control flow, useful zero values, and small helper logic. If your solution has more ceremony than the problem statement, simplify it.
hints: Say the function signature in English before coding.
Write the zero/empty case first.
Trace the first sample manually and make your variables follow that trace.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(a,b string) bool { m:=map[rune]int{}; for _,r:=range a {m[r]++}; for _,r:=range b {m[r]--}; for _,v:=range m { if v!=0 {return false} }; return true }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve("silent","listen"), true); assertEqual(Solve("go","goo"), false); assertEqual(Solve("夜猫","猫夜"), true) }
problems.lesson:

Skipping edge cases: empty input, one item, duplicates, negative numbers, or Unicode depending on the problem.

problems.title: Rotate Slice
problems.statement: Rotate a slice left by k positions and return a new slice.
problems.explanation:
Intuition first: this problem is asking you to model one tiny transformation. The input is not mysterious; it is just data waiting to be reshaped into the return value.

A good Go solution here avoids hiding the important state. If you need a running answer, name it something like total, out, seen, or best. If you need to remember membership, use a map. If you need to preserve order, keep a slice. If you need to inspect text, decide whether byte-level or rune-level processing is correct.

The reference solution is short because the data structure does most of the work. Read it from top to bottom and track exactly how the state changes for the first example. That trace is the real explanation.

Why this is idiomatic Go: concrete types, explicit control flow, useful zero values, and small helper logic. If your solution has more ceremony than the problem statement, simplify it.
hints: Say the function signature in English before coding.
Write the zero/empty case first.
Trace the first sample manually and make your variables follow that trace.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(nums []int,k int) []int { n:=len(nums); if n==0 {return []int{}}; k%=n; out:=append([]int{}, nums[k:]...); out=append(out, nums[:k]...); return out }
problems.lesson:

problems.title: Nested List Depth
problems.statement: Given a JSON-like nested integer list encoded as interface values, compute max nesting depth.
problems.explanation:
Intuition first: this problem is asking you to model one tiny transformation. The input is not mysterious; it is just data waiting to be reshaped into the return value.

A good Go solution here avoids hiding the important state. If you need a running answer, name it something like total, out, seen, or best. If you need to remember membership, use a map. If you need to preserve order, keep a slice. If you need to inspect text, decide whether byte-level or rune-level processing is correct.

The reference solution is short because the data structure does most of the work. Read it from top to bottom and track exactly how the state changes for the first example. That trace is the real explanation.

Why this is idiomatic Go: concrete types, explicit control flow, useful zero values, and small helper logic. If your solution has more ceremony than the problem statement, simplify it.
hints: Say the function signature in English before coding.
Write the zero/empty case first.
Trace the first sample manually and make your variables follow that trace.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(v any) int { switch x:=v.(type){ case []any: best:=1; for _,e:=range x { if d:=1+Solve(e); d>best {best=d} }; return best; default: return 0 } }
problems.lessonTitle: Why this matters: Nested List Depth
problems.lesson:
This exercise belongs to Functions.

problems.title: Deferred Product
problems.statement: Process numbers left-to-right, but multiply positive numbers as if deferred in reverse; return the same final product.
problems.explanation:
Intuition first: this problem is asking you to model one tiny transformation. The input is not mysterious; it is just data waiting to be reshaped into the return value.

A good Go solution here avoids hiding the important state. If you need a running answer, name it something like total, out, seen, or best. If you need to remember membership, use a map. If you need to preserve order, keep a slice. If you need to inspect text, decide whether byte-level or rune-level processing is correct.

The reference solution is short because the data structure does most of the work. Read it from top to bottom and track exactly how the state changes for the first example. That trace is the real explanation.

Why this is idiomatic Go: concrete types, explicit control flow, useful zero values, and small helper logic. If your solution has more ceremony than the problem statement, simplify it.
hints: Say the function signature in English before coding.
Write the zero/empty case first.
Trace the first sample manually and make your variables follow that trace.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(nums []int) int { p:=1; for _,x:=range nums { if x>0 {p*=x} }; return p }
problems.lesson:
This exercise belongs to Functions.

The big idea: Function design, errors, recursion, closures, panic/recover, and defer.

Do not treat this like random syntax practice. Treat it like learning a mental move. Every Go problem has three visible things: the data coming in, the state you keep while working, and the data going out. If you can name those three, the code usually becomes short.

For this problem, stare at the function signature first. The parameter types tell you what world you are living in. A []int means order matters. A map means lookup/counting matters. A string means you must ask: bytes or runes? A struct means fields have meaning.

Your study goal: solve it once slowly and clearly. Then solve it again with fewer moving parts. Idiomatic Go usually feels calm: small variables, explicit loops, boring names, and no magic.
problems.approach:
Attack plan for Deferred Product:

1. Identify the zero/empty case before writing the main logic.
2. Choose the smallest useful state variable. Do not create a framework for a 10-line function.
3. Walk through one sample by hand and make your variable changes match that story.
4. Only after it works, ask if the code reads like plain English.

For this specific task, the core move is: Process numbers left-to-right, but multiply positive numbers as if deferred in reverse; return the same final product. Keep the implementation direct; the judge rewards correctness and clarity, not cleverness.
pitfalls: Starting with code before understanding the input/output shape.
Using a clever abstraction where a plain loop or map would be clearer.
Skipping edge cases: empty input, one item, duplicates, negative numbers, or Unicode depending on the problem.

problems.title: Point Manhattan
problems.statement: Implement a method-style helper concept: return Manhattan distance between two points.
problems.explanation:
Intuition first: this problem is asking you to model one tiny transformation. The input is not mysterious; it is just data waiting to be reshaped into the return value.

A good Go solution here avoids hiding the important state. If you need a running answer, name it something like total, out, seen, or best. If you need to remember membership, use a map. If you need to preserve order, keep a slice. If you need to inspect text, decide whether byte-level or rune-level processing is correct.

The reference solution is short because the data structure does most of the work. Read it from top to bottom and track exactly how the state changes for the first example. That trace is the real explanation.

Why this is idiomatic Go: concrete types, explicit control flow, useful zero values, and small helper logic. If your solution has more ceremony than the problem statement, simplify it.

Write the zero/empty case first.
Trace the first sample manually and make your variables follow that trace.

func Solve(chunks []string) int { n:=0; for _,s:=range chunks {n+=len([]byte(s))}; return n }

problems.title: Merge Sorted Streams
problems.statement: Merge two sorted integer streams into one sorted slice.
problems.explanation:
Intuition first: this problem is asking you to model one tiny transformation. The input is not mysterious; it is just data waiting to be reshaped into the return value.

A good Go solution here avoids hiding the important state. If you need a running answer, name it something like total, out, seen, or best. If you need to remember membership, use a map. If you need to preserve order, keep a slice. If you need to inspect text, decide whether byte-level or rune-level processing is correct.

The reference solution is short because the data structure does most of the work. Read it from top to bottom and track exactly how the state changes for the first example. That trace is the real explanation.

Why this is idiomatic Go: concrete types, explicit control flow, useful zero values, and small helper logic. If your solution has more ceremony than the problem statement, simplify it.
hints: Say the function signature in English before coding.
Write the zero/empty case first.
Trace the first sample manually and make your variables follow that trace.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(a,b []int) []int { i,j:=0,0; out:=[]int{}; for i<len(a)||j<len(b){ if j==len(b)||(i<len(a)&&a[i]<=b[j]){out=append(out,a[i]); i++}else{out=append(out,b[j]); j++} }; return out }
problems.lessonTitle: Why this matters: Merge Sorted Streams
problems.lesson:

The big idea: Goroutines, channels, pipelines, cancellation, fan-in/fan-out.

problems.title: Safe Counter
problems.statement: Given increments and decrements, return the final counter value.
problems.lesson:
This exercise belongs to Shared Variables and Mutexes.

The big idea: Race-free shared state, mutexes, once-only init, and confinement.

Think of this as a lab exercise, not a trivia question. The point is to build an instinct: what kind of state does this situation need, and who owns that state?

Before coding, draw a tiny box for the current value you care about. If the value changes over time, ask what protects it. If the value is just being transformed, ask what the clean return shape should be.

Your goal is not to memorize a trick. Your goal is to make the invisible rule visible in code.
problems.approach:
Attack plan:

1. Translate the requirement into one invariant: what should be true after each loop step?
2. Pick the smallest state that preserves that invariant.
3. Let Go zero values help when they are correct, but initialize from real data when zero would lie.
4. Return the final state.

Core move: Given increments and decrements, return the final counter value.
pitfalls: Confusing the concept with unnecessary machinery.
Forgetting empty input behavior.
Returning nondeterministic output when sorted/stable output is expected.
hints: Name the invariant before naming variables.
Prefer one clear owner for mutable state.
Make output deterministic if maps are involved.
problems.explanation:
This problem is a small version of a real Go design decision. The solution works because it keeps the important state obvious and avoids pretending the problem is bigger than it is.

Read the reference solution as a story: initialize the state, update it once per input item, return it. That pattern appears everywhere in Go: CLIs, servers, tests, parsers, and concurrent workers.

If you can explain why each variable exists, you understood the concept. If a variable feels decorative, remove it.
problems.solutionCode: func Solve(ops []int) int { total:=0; for _,v:=range ops { total+=v }; return total }

problems.title: Initialize Once Max
problems.statement: Return the maximum number, or 0 for empty input.
problems.lessonTitle: Why this matters: Initialize Once Max
problems.lesson:

If you can explain why each variable exists, you understood the concept. If a variable feels decorative, remove it.
problems.solutionCode: func Solve(nums []int) int { if len(nums)==0{return 0}; m:=nums[0]; for _,x:=range nums[1:] { if x>m {m=x} }; return m }

problems.title: Import Cleaner
problems.statement: Return unique non-empty import paths sorted alphabetically.
problems.lesson:
This exercise belongs to Packages and the Go Tool.

The big idea: Package design, imports, internal APIs, modules, and build/test workflows.

Think of this as a lab exercise, not a trivia question. The point is to build an instinct: what kind of state does this situation need, and who owns that state?

Before coding, draw a tiny box for the current value you care about. If the value changes over time, ask what protects it. If the value is just being transformed, ask what the clean return shape should be.

Your goal is not to memorize a trick. Your goal is to make the invisible rule visible in code.

2. Pick the smallest state that preserves that invariant.
3. Let Go zero values help when they are correct, but initialize from real data when zero would lie.
4. Return the final state.

Core move: Return unique non-empty import paths sorted alphabetically.
pitfalls: Confusing the concept with unnecessary machinery.
Forgetting empty input behavior.
Returning nondeterministic output when sorted/stable output is expected.
hints: Name the invariant before naming variables.
Prefer one clear owner for mutable state.
Make output deterministic if maps are involved.
problems.explanation:
This problem is a small version of a real Go design decision. The solution works because it keeps the important state obvious and avoids pretending the problem is bigger than it is.

Read the reference solution as a story: initialize the state, update it once per input item, return it. That pattern appears everywhere in Go: CLIs, servers, tests, parsers, and concurrent workers.

If you can explain why each variable exists, you understood the concept. If a variable feels decorative, remove it.
problems.solutionCode:
import "sort"
func Solve(paths []string) []string { m:=map[string]bool{}; for _,p:=range paths { if p!="" {m[p]=true} }; out:=[]string{}; for p:=range m {out=append(out,p)}; sort.Strings(out); return out }

problems.title: Module Path Parts
problems.statement: Split a module path on slashes and remove empty parts.
problems.lessonTitle: Why this matters: Module Path Parts

problems.solutionCode:
import "strings"
func Solve(path string) []string { raw:=strings.Split(path,"/"); out:=[]string{}; for _,p:=range raw { if p!="" {out=append(out,p)} }; return out }

problems.solutionCode:
import "strings"
func Solve(s string) string { return strings.Join(strings.Fields(s)," ") }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve("  go   is fun "),"go is fun"); assertEqual(Solve(""),"") }

Core move: Return the reflect kind name for a dynamic value, or invalid for nil.
pitfalls: Confusing the concept with unnecessary machinery.
Forgetting empty input behavior.
Returning nondeterministic output when sorted/stable output is expected.
hints: Name the invariant before naming variables.
Prefer one clear owner for mutable state.
Make output deterministic if maps are involved.
problems.explanation:
This problem is a small version of a real Go design decision. The solution works because it keeps the important state obvious and avoids pretending the problem is bigger than it is.

Read the reference solution as a story: initialize the state, update it once per input item, return it. That pattern appears everywhere in Go: CLIs, servers, tests, parsers, and concurrent workers.

If you can explain why each variable exists, you understood the concept. If a variable feels decorative, remove it.
problems.solutionCode:
import "reflect"
func Solve(v any) string { if v==nil {return "invalid"}; return reflect.TypeOf(v).Kind().String() }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve(3),"int"); assertEqual(Solve("x"),"string"); assertEqual(Solve([]int{}),"slice"); assertEqual(Solve(nil),"invalid") }

problems.title: Struct Tag Lookup
problems.statement: Return the json tag for a named struct field, or empty string.
problems.lessonTitle: Why this matters: Struct Tag Lookup
problems.lesson:
This exercise belongs to Reflection.

The big idea: Inspecting dynamic values, tags, generic encoders, and reflect pitfalls.

Think of this as a lab exercise, not a trivia question. The point is to build an instinct: what kind of state does this situation need, and who owns that state?

Before coding, draw a tiny box for the current value you care about. If the value changes over time, ask what protects it. If the value is just being transformed, ask what the clean return shape should be.

Your goal is not to memorize a trick. Your goal is to make the invisible rule visible in code.
problems.approach:
Attack plan:

1. Translate the requirement into one invariant: what should be true after each loop step?
2. Pick the smallest state that preserves that invariant.
3. Let Go zero values help when they are correct, but initialize from real data when zero would lie.
4. Return the final state.

Core move: Return the json tag for a named struct field, or empty string.
pitfalls: Confusing the concept with unnecessary machinery.
Forgetting empty input behavior.
Returning nondeterministic output when sorted/stable output is expected.
hints: Name the invariant before naming variables.
Prefer one clear owner for mutable state.
Make output deterministic if maps are involved.
problems.explanation:
This problem is a small version of a real Go design decision. The solution works because it keeps the important state obvious and avoids pretending the problem is bigger than it is.

Read the reference solution as a story: initialize the state, update it once per input item, return it. That pattern appears everywhere in Go: CLIs, servers, tests, parsers, and concurrent workers.

If you can explain why each variable exists, you understood the concept. If a variable feels decorative, remove it.
problems.solutionCode:
import "reflect"
func Solve(v any, field string) string { t:=reflect.TypeOf(v); if t==nil {return ""}; if t.Kind()==reflect.Pointer {t=t.Elem()}; if t.Kind()!=reflect.Struct {return ""}; f,ok:=t.FieldByName(field); if !ok {return ""}; return f.Tag.Get("json") }
problems.testCode:
type User struct{Name string `json:"name"`; Age int `json:"age"`}
func main(){ assertEqual(Solve(User{},"Name"),"name"); assertEqual(Solve(&User{},"Age"),"age"); assertEqual(Solve(3,"Name"),"") }

problems.title: Byte View
problems.statement: Return the byte values of an ASCII string.
problems.lesson:
This exercise belongs to Low-Level Programming.

The big idea: Unsafe, memory layout, alignment, byte views, and when not to be clever.

Think of this as a lab exercise, not a trivia question. The point is to build an instinct: what kind of state does this situation need, and who owns that state?

Before coding, draw a tiny box for the current value you care about. If the value changes over time, ask what protects it. If the value is just being transformed, ask what the clean return shape should be.

Your goal is not to memorize a trick. Your goal is to make the invisible rule visible in code.
problems.approach:
Attack plan:

1. Translate the requirement into one invariant: what should be true after each loop step?
2. Pick the smallest state that preserves that invariant.
3. Let Go zero values help when they are correct, but initialize from real data when zero would lie.
4. Return the final state.

Core move: Return the byte values of an ASCII string.
pitfalls: Confusing the concept with unnecessary machinery.
Forgetting empty input behavior.
Returning nondeterministic output when sorted/stable output is expected.
hints: Name the invariant before naming variables.
Prefer one clear owner for mutable state.
Make output deterministic if maps are involved.
problems.explanation:
This problem is a small version of a real Go design decision. The solution works because it keeps the important state obvious and avoids pretending the problem is bigger than it is.

Read the reference solution as a story: initialize the state, update it once per input item, return it. That pattern appears everywhere in Go: CLIs, servers, tests, parsers, and concurrent workers.

If you can explain why each variable exists, you understood the concept. If a variable feels decorative, remove it.
problems.solutionCode: func Solve(s string) []byte { return []byte(s) }

problems.title: Pair Packing
problems.statement: Given field sizes, add padding so total is aligned to 2 after each field.
problems.lesson:
This exercise belongs to Low-Level Programming.

The big idea: Unsafe, memory layout, alignment, byte views, and when not to be clever.

Think of this as a lab exercise, not a trivia question. The point is to build an instinct: what kind of state does this situation need, and who owns that state?

Before coding, draw a tiny box for the current value you care about. If the value changes over time, ask what protects it. If the value is just being transformed, ask what the clean return shape should be.

Your goal is not to memorize a trick. Your goal is to make the invisible rule visible in code.
problems.approach:
Attack plan:

1. Translate the requirement into one invariant: what should be true after each loop step?
2. Pick the smallest state that preserves that invariant.
3. Let Go zero values help when they are correct, but initialize from real data when zero would lie.
4. Return the final state.

Core move: Given field sizes, add padding so total is aligned to 2 after each field.
pitfalls: Confusing the concept with unnecessary machinery.
Forgetting empty input behavior.
Returning nondeterministic output when sorted/stable output is expected.
hints: Name the invariant before naming variables.
Prefer one clear owner for mutable state.
Make output deterministic if maps are involved.
problems.explanation:
This problem is a small version of a real Go design decision. The solution works because it keeps the important state obvious and avoids pretending the problem is bigger than it is.

Read the reference solution as a story: initialize the state, update it once per input item, return it. That pattern appears everywhere in Go: CLIs, servers, tests, parsers, and concurrent workers.

If you can explain why each variable exists, you understood the concept. If a variable feels decorative, remove it.
problems.solutionCode: func Solve(sizes []int) int { total:=0; for _,x:=range sizes { total+=x; if total%2!=0 {total++} }; return total }
